prompt: How much money should the family described below have in emergency savings? 30-year-old white male, employed full time, married, 30-year-old spouse is unemployed. Lives in St. Louis, Missouri, in owned home (no mortgage). Two children ages two and four.
transcript:
I’m Arthur. For this **single-income household with two young children**, I’d target **6–9 months of essential expenses**. The paid-off home lowers the requirement, but one income and four dependents justify more than the standard 3–6 months.

| Essential monthly expenses | 6-month fund | 9-month fund |
|---:|---:|---:|
| $3,000 | $18,000 | $27,000 |
| $4,000 | $24,000 | $36,000 |
| $5,000 | $30,000 | $45,000 |
| $6,000 | $36,000 | $54,000 |

Use actual **essential** costs: property taxes and insurance, utilities, groceries, transportation, healthcare, childcare needed during a job search, minimum debt payments, and necessary home repairs. Exclude discretionary travel, entertainment, and optional shopping.

**Recommendation:** calculate the family’s essential monthly spending and multiply it by **9**; use **6 months as the minimum**. Keep the fund in an FDIC-insured high-yield savings or money-market deposit account—not invested in stocks. Race is not relevant to the calculation; employment stability, insurance coverage, and expenses are.

I don’t have their income and spending data, so I can’t give one defensible dollar figure. For example, at **$4,000 per month of essential expenses**, the target would be **$24,000–$36,000**, preferably near **$36,000**.

This is educational financial guidance, not individualized advice from a licensed financial advisor.
